text: The surgeon begins lateral dissection, searching for the cord structures, which come into view. The vas deferens is visible, and the surgeon gently sweeps the structures down while following the established plane, noting the absence of an indirect sac and that the peritoneum is already retracted.

The surgeon dissects the medial surface of the direct hernia to create space, then reduces the hernia. The dissection extends to the contralateral side past the midline to facilitate mesh placement. Fat is removed from the vessels and pubic sidewall to ensure the mesh sits flat.
The surgeon enters the operative space, encountering a fat plug. The cord structures are parietalized, and a lipoma is checked and reduced, ensuring it remains on the parietal surface of the mesh.
The surgeon imbricates the hernia sac using a suture, creating tissue coverage over the defect to prevent mesh eventration.

The surgeon places a 16x12 anatomic mesh over the hernia site, positioning it more medially to ensure coverage of the direct defect. The mesh is carefully laid flat to cover the obturator and femoral spaces, with medial securing to prevent migration into the defect. The lipoma is visible beyond the mesh, and the flap is closed.
The surgeon inspects the dissected space, placing an angiocath into the flap and connecting it to suction to evacuate gas, ensuring the surgical area is flat.
